title: Disputed Venezuelan presidential election
summary: On Sunday 28 July 2024, Venezuelans voted in a presidential election framed by both government and opposition as decisive for the country's future. The incumbent Nicolas Maduro, in office since the death of Hugo Chavez in 2013, faced Edmundo Gonzalez Urrutia, a mild-mannered career diplomat drafted late by the opposition after the Chavista-controlled Supreme Court blocked the front-runner Maria Corina Machado from running. In the early hours of 29 July the government-controlled National Electoral Council (CNE) declared Maduro re-elected with 51.2 percent of the vote and Gonzalez on 44.2 percent. It refused, then and in the months that followed, to release the constitutionally-required precinct-level tally sheets. Within days the opposition published copies of more than 80 percent of those sheets, collected by its poll-watchers, showing Gonzalez winning by roughly 67 to 30 percent. The Carter Center, one of the few independent observers, declared that the election 'cannot be considered democratic'. Protests erupted across the country and were met with mass arrests. Maduro was inaugurated on 10 January 2025 for a third six-year term over the objections of the United States, the European Union, most Latin American democracies and much of Venezuelan civil society. Gonzalez, facing arrest, fled to Spain; Machado went into hiding inside Venezuela. She was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in October 2025.

sections.heading: Background

image.caption: Nicolas Maduro, in office since March 2013, sought a third six-year term in the July 2024 election.

sections.body:
Nicolas Maduro, a former bus driver and trade-union official who rose through the Chavista political apparatus to serve as Hugo Chavez's foreign minister and vice-president, became acting president on Chavez's death from cancer on 5 March 2013. He was narrowly elected in his own right the following month over the opposition candidate Henrique Capriles, in an election Capriles refused to accept. Maduro's first full term coincided with the collapse of world oil prices, on which the Venezuelan economy depended for more than 95 percent of its export earnings; by the late 2010s the country was experiencing the world's worst peacetime economic contraction in living memory, with hyperinflation peaking above one million percent in 2018, GDP contracting by more than three-quarters, and mass emigration eventually reaching an estimated eight million people, or roughly a quarter of the population.

Maduro was re-elected in May 2018 in a poll boycotted by most of the opposition and rejected by much of the international community as neither free nor fair. In January 2019 the National Assembly leader Juan Guaido invoked a constitutional provision to declare himself acting president; the United States, most Latin American democracies and much of Europe recognised him, but Guaido never succeeded in dislodging Maduro, who retained the loyalty of the military high command and the crucial support of Russia, China, Iran and Cuba. By 2023 the Guaido experiment had collapsed and Western sanctions relief was on offer in exchange for a competitive 2024 election.

On 17 October 2023 the Maduro government and the opposition Unitary Platform signed the Barbados Agreement, brokered by Norway and the United States, providing for the release of political prisoners, the readmission of banned candidates, and independent observation of the 2024 vote. On the same day Maria Corina Machado, a fifty-six-year-old industrial engineer and former legislator with a long record of opposition to Chavismo, won an opposition primary with 92 percent. Within days, and in direct violation of Barbados, the Chavista-controlled Comptroller General ruled Machado disqualified from public office for fifteen years. The Supreme Tribunal upheld the ban in January 2024. When it became clear that the opposition would not be permitted to run any Machado-approved substitute either, they nominated Edmundo Gonzalez Urrutia, a seventy-four-year-old career diplomat whose last significant post had been ambassador to Argentina two decades earlier, as their placeholder candidate. Machado campaigned tirelessly across the country for the ticket, drawing enormous crowds despite constant harassment.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: Edmundo Gonzalez Urrutia, the seventy-four-year-old former diplomat who became the opposition presidential candidate.
image.credit: Adriana Nunez Rabascall / Voice of America

sections.body:
Polling day on 28 July 2024 - chosen by Maduro to coincide with the seventieth anniversary of Chavez's birth - was long and, in most polling stations, remarkably orderly. Turnout was heavy despite oppressive summer heat and government-orchestrated last-minute changes to polling stations that disenfranchised many voters overseas. Opposition witnesses were present at the great majority of the country's approximately 30,000 polling stations. Voting machines produced paper tally sheets, or actas, at the close of poll; by law these were to be given to authorised witnesses, transmitted to the CNE, and displayed publicly at each station. Independent quick-count operations, most notably one run by the Edison Research analytics firm on behalf of AP, indicated an opposition landslide in the range of two to one.

As the polls closed, the CNE's normally routine transmission of preliminary results was delayed. In the early hours of 29 July, CNE president Elvis Amoroso, a longtime Chavista loyalist, appeared on state television to announce that with 80 percent of ballots counted Maduro had won 51.2 percent to Gonzalez's 44.2 percent, and to declare Maduro re-elected. The CNE cited an unspecified 'hacking' attack for its refusal to release the actas required by Venezuelan electoral law. The opposition immediately called the result fraudulent and produced, in the following seventy-two hours, digital copies of more than 24,000 tally sheets from more than 80 percent of national polling stations, uploaded to a public website. Aggregated, these showed Gonzalez winning some 67.05 percent to Maduro's 30.46 percent. The Carter Center, which had a small mission on the ground, concluded on 30 July that the election 'did not meet international standards of electoral integrity and cannot be considered democratic'; a preliminary report by the UN Panel of Electoral Experts reached a similar conclusion. The European Union electoral mission had been disinvited earlier in the year.

Protests erupted almost immediately, above all in the poor and previously Chavista neighbourhoods of Caracas and other cities. Government crackdowns, under the rubric of a security campaign that Maduro named 'Operation Tun Tun' ('knock knock'), were rapid and brutal. Human rights groups documented more than two thousand arrests in the first week of August, including of minors, and at least twenty-eight killings. Maria Corina Machado went into hiding within Venezuela but continued to lead the opposition through video appearances and social media. Edmundo Gonzalez, after weeks of pressure and a court-issued arrest warrant, boarded a Spanish military aircraft on 7 September 2024 and flew to Madrid, where he was granted political asylum. Maduro was inaugurated for a third term on 10 January 2025 at a Caracas ceremony boycotted by almost every Western democracy.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences

image.caption: Protesters in a Venezuelan street in the weeks after the 28 July 2024 election.

sections.body:
The international response fractured along familiar lines. The United States and the European Union declined to recognise the CNE's declared results. Panama, Peru, Argentina, Ecuador, the Dominican Republic and Costa Rica recognised Gonzalez as president-elect within weeks. Brazil, Colombia and Mexico - governed by leftist presidents Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, Gustavo Petro and Andres Manuel Lopez Obrador who had been expected to lend Maduro cover - broke sharply with Caracas by insisting on the release of the disaggregated actas as a condition for recognising any result, a demand the Maduro government continued to refuse. On the other side, Russia, China, Iran, Cuba, Nicaragua and North Korea recognised Maduro without qualification. The Organization of American States failed to reach a consensus on a resolution condemning the process.

Inside Venezuela the repression that followed the election was on a larger scale than in previous cycles. The Attorney General Tarek William Saab opened criminal investigations of Machado, Gonzalez, and much of the senior opposition leadership. Journalists, human rights lawyers, and Colombian and Argentine citizens working in Venezuela were arrested; several foreign nationals were held for months as bargaining chips. UN High Commissioner for Human Rights Volker Turk warned in early 2025 of a 'systematic and widespread' pattern of persecution. The exodus of Venezuelans northward through the Darien Gap on the Colombia-Panama border and onward toward the United States, which had partially slowed under Biden-era migration deals, resumed at higher pace through 2025.

The Maduro government's second consecutive election crisis coincided with the return of Donald Trump to the White House in January 2025 and a sharp escalation in U.S. policy toward the Caribbean and Central America. In 2025 and 2026 the second Trump administration reimposed and expanded oil-sector sanctions, deployed U.S. Navy warships and aircraft in the Caribbean, and launched a series of strikes on vessels alleged to be Venezuelan drug-trafficking boats. The scale of the U.S. military presence off Venezuela in early 2026 was without precedent since the 1989 Panama invasion. Whether that pressure would produce a Maduro exit, a negotiated transition, or a wider Caribbean conflict was, at this writing in mid-2026, the great open question of the crisis.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The 2024 Venezuelan election was in one sense simply the latest instalment of a long autocratic consolidation, but in another it was a landmark. Never before had the Venezuelan opposition been so unified, so well-organised at the precinct level, and so effective at demonstrating - to Venezuelans and to the world - the divergence between the government's claimed result and the aggregated evidence of the actas. The parallel vote-count based on distributed poll-watcher data, and the immediate public release of scanned tally sheets, has been noted by election-integrity practitioners as a model that may spread to other partial democracies confronting authoritarian incumbents.

For Latin America the crisis has been unusually clarifying. The regional left, long divided over how to handle Chavismo, has largely accepted that the Maduro government cannot be defended on democratic grounds; even Lula and Petro have, while stopping short of recognising Gonzalez, refused to attend the January 2025 inauguration. The traditional left-right cleavages of Latin American politics have been overlaid, sharpened and in some cases realigned by the Venezuela question. The regional migration crisis, meanwhile - by early 2026 Venezuelans were the second-largest displaced population in the world after Syrians - has become one of the defining social and political issues from Bogota to Buenos Aires to Miami.

Maria Corina Machado's award of the Nobel Peace Prize by the Norwegian Nobel Committee on 10 October 2025, delivered while she was still in hiding inside Venezuela, capped a remarkable year of international recognition. In the citation the committee praised her 'tireless work promoting democratic rights for the people of Venezuela'. Whether the recognition would translate into a change on the ground remained uncertain. The precedent of Aung San Suu Kyi, whose 1991 Nobel had preceded her country's democratic opening by decades, and the more sobering precedent of Belarus's Sviatlana Tsikhanouskaya, still in exile years after her own contested election, both hung over the Venezuelan case.

figures.name: Nicolas Maduro
figures.role: President of Venezuela since March 2013; declared re-elected for a third term by the CNE on 29 July 2024 without the release of precinct-level tally sheets
figures.credit: Ricardo Stuckert / Palacio do Planalto, CC BY 2.0

figures.name: Edmundo Gonzalez Urrutia
figures.role: Career Venezuelan diplomat and opposition presidential candidate; opposition tally sheets showed him winning some 67 percent of the vote; fled to Spain in September 2024

figures.name: Maria Corina Machado
figures.role: Opposition primary winner barred from running by the Chavista-controlled courts; led the opposition ground campaign for Gonzalez; awarded the 2025 Nobel Peace Prize

figures.name: Elvis Amoroso
figures.role: President of the Chavista-controlled National Electoral Council (CNE); announced the disputed results and blocked the release of precinct-level actas

figures.name: Diosdado Cabello
figures.role: Longtime senior PSUV figure and Maduro loyalist; appointed interior minister in the wake of the election and effectively directed the post-vote crackdown

images.caption: Maria Corina Machado at a press conference in Valencia, Venezuela, October 2023, days before winning the opposition primary.
